comment.body: First, they’re not selling debt back to the US gov’t.  They’re selling to other entities.  Second, you should think of it in terms of supply and demand.  If they start selling, that’s more supply that needs to be absorbed and prices could go down (yields and this interest rates go up).  Plus if they were net buyers in the past and they are no longer buying, then demand goes down so price is getting hit on both sides of the supply/demand curves.
comment.raw_body: First, they’re not selling debt back to the US gov’t.  They’re selling to other entities.  Second, you should think of it in terms of supply and demand.  If they start selling, that’s more supply that needs to be absorbed and prices could go down (yields and this interest rates go up).  Plus if they were net buyers in the past and they are no longer buying, then demand goes down so price is getting hit on both sides of the supply/demand curves.

comment.body:
Going short volatility in a short-term spot-up/consolidation environment gives one several options.  As for me, I’m sitting on a fair amount of cash so I sold cash-secured puts on stocks I’d like to own at prices I wouldn’t mind paying for them.  If the price goes up and/or IV goes down, then I’ll buy back the puts at a profit.  If the price goes down, then I was prepared to buy the stock at that price anyway.

So there are some ways to play this.  That’s just one.
